risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer-generated sequential list contained the study numbers linked to treatment identification letters, randomly ordered in blocks of 10. ... Women were randomised to receive 3 tablets of SP ... or placebo." Comment: This describes a computer-generated random sequence with random permuted blocks, which is a proper random allocation method. The presence of "computer-generated" and "randomly ordered in blocks of 10" supports Low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: "After written informed consent was obtained, the lowest available study number was assigned. A computer-generated sequential list contained the study numbers linked to treatment identification letters, randomly ordered in blocks of 10. Tablets of SP or placebo, identical in shape and colour, were stored in 10 bottles labelled only with a single treatment identification letter. Women were randomised to receive 3 tablets of SP (1500 mg sulphadoxine/75 mg pyrimetamine) or placebo."
_evidence.method_summary: Computer-generated sequential list with treatment letters, randomly ordered in blocks of 10; participants were randomised to SP or placebo.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After written informed consent was obtained, the lowest available study number was assigned. A computer-generated sequential list contained the study numbers linked to treatment identification letters, randomly ordered in blocks of 10. Tablets of SP or placebo, identical in shape and colour, were stored in 10 bottles labelled only with a single treatment identification letter." Comment: The article explicitly describes a mechanism that prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment: participants were assigned the lowest available study number, and treatment identification was kept separate in identical, letter-labelled bottles. This indicates allocation concealment was maintained for the recruiter/enroller.

_evidence.concealment_quote: After written informed consent was obtained, the lowest available study number was assigned. A computer-generated sequential list contained the study numbers linked to treatment identification letters, randomly ordered in blocks of 10. Tablets of SP or placebo, identical in shape and colour, were stored in 10 bottles labelled only with a single treatment identification letter.
_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation and a blinded drug-labeling scheme, but does not mention envelopes; treatment bottles were identical in shape and colour and labelled only with a single treatment identification letter.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This double blind, individually randomised, placebo controlled trial" and "Tablets of SP or placebo, identical in shape and colour, were stored in 10 bottles labelled only with a single treatment identification letter." Comment: The study explicitly states that both participants and personnel were blinded using matching placebo tablets and coded bottles, making knowledge of allocation unlikely to have influenced care or outcomes.

_evidence.blinding_quote: This double blind, individually randomised, placebo controlled trial had the primary objective of estimating the additive protective effect of two-dose IPTp with SP to that of ITNs on LBW prevalence.
_evidence.blinding_method: Tablets of SP or placebo, identical in shape and colour, were stored in 10 bottles labelled only with a single treatment identification letter.
outcome_categories: malaria parasitaemia at 8 weeks after delivery
women or infants with clinical malaria or anaemia
subjective_outcomes: women or infants with clinical malaria or anaemia
objective_outcomes: malaria parasitaemia at 8 weeks after delivery

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This double blind, individually randomised, placebo controlled trial..." Comment: The paper states the trial was double blind, but it does not clearly say who assessed outcomes or explicitly confirm that outcome assessors were blinded. Although most outcomes are objective (e.g. birth weight, lab-based parasitology, haemoglobin/PCV), the study also reports subjective/clinician-dependent outcomes such as "clinical malaria" and "outpatient visits / hospital admissions". Because assessor blinding is not explicitly described and at least one outcome is judgement-dependent, blinding of outcome assessment cannot be confirmed as low risk, but there is also no positive evidence that assessors were unblinded.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: This double blind, individually randomised, placebo controlled trial had the primary objective of estimating the additive protective effect of two-dose IPTp with SP to that of ITNs on LBW prevalence.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Data were analysed by intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis whereby all randomised women were included regardless of whether or not they had received the intervention and the number of doses." Comment: The article states ITT, but the supplied text does not provide per-arm numbers analysed vs lost for the outcome time points, nor reasons for any post-randomization missing data. Although some outcome denominators differ from 515 (e.g. 496 vs 494 for birth weight; 432 vs 416 for parasitaemia), the missingness pattern cannot be fully quantified from the provided text, so attrition bias cannot be judged as low or high from reported evidence alone.

_evidence.notes: Article states: "Data were analysed by intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis whereby all randomised women were included regardless of whether or not they had received the intervention and the number of doses." CONSORT-style flow is referenced in Figure 1, but the provided text/tables here do not include per-arm analysed or lost counts or dropout reasons.
